title: First-class / apply'd builtin equality predicates return raw int (0/1) or () instead of #f/#t
status_note: MERGED #86 / origin/master 7affe0d2. Fix packs first-class comparison results as booleans, adds first-class wrappers for eq?/eqv?/equal?, and teaches apply to resolve predicate wrappers. Non-windows-arm64 CI green; windows-arm64 tracked separately.
goal: Found by the SICP gate (real root cause of the ch4 metacircular evaluator failure). A builtin equality/identity predicate (=, and likely eq?/eqv?/equal?) invoked as a FIRST-CLASS VALUE (extracted from a list/env) or via apply returns the raw integer 0/1 (or () for apply) instead of the boolean #f/#t. DIRECT named calls are correct, and notably (<) first-class IS correct (#t) — so the first-class wrapper/apply path for = (and the other equality preds) mis-packs the boolean result, while comparison preds like < pack it correctly. Since only #f is falsy in Scheme, a 0 result is TRUTHY, which silently breaks higher-order predicate use (filter/map/apply with =, and the SICP metacircular evaluator's (if (not (eq? (= n 0) #f)) ...) which always took the base case → fact 5 = 1).
root_cause_hint: The first-class value wrapper for = / equality predicates (and the apply dispatch) returns the comparison as an int (or null) tagged value instead of packing it as a boolean. Mirror the correct < first-class wrapper. Look at createBuiltin*Function / the first-class predicate dispatch in lib/backend/llvm_codegen.cpp and the apply/codegenClosureCall path for builtins.
acceptance: (cmp 2 0)=#f, (cmp 2 2)=#t for cmp=(car (list =)); (apply = (list 2 0))=#f; eq?/eqv?/equal? first-class + apply return booleans; (< > <= >=) unaffected; -r AND AOT; tests/sicp/ch4_metacircular.esk passes (fact 5=120)
campaign_payoff: Unblocks higher-order predicate use across the language AND the SICP ch4 metacircular evaluator (currently XFAIL) — and ch5.4/5.5 which the SICP agent found blocked behind it.
